samples.claim: The Solid-state drive article on Wikipedia explains that SSDs use NAND flash memory as their persistent storage medium.

The NAND flash memory article details how these cells operate: a voltage applied to the control gate causes electrons to tunnel through the oxide layer via **Fowler-Nordheim tunneling**, becoming trap

The Multi-level cell Wikipedia article explicitly states that with current technology, a maximum lifetime of up to 3,000 program/erase cycles is typical for triple-level cell (TLC) NAND, while single-
The Flash memory article explains that flash memory must be erased before it can be written to, and this erase operation is performed on large blocks rather than individual cells.
The Data integrity article discusses how storage systems must maintain data correctness despite physical degradation mechanisms.
The Multi-level cell article confirms that TLC NAND has a maximum lifetime of up to 3,000 P/E cycles.
The Write amplification article on Wikipedia explains this concept in detail.
Garbage collection (computer science)) is the process by which the SSD controller reclaims space occupied by invalid (stale) data.
The TRIM (computing)) command is an ATA interface command that allows the operating system to inform the SSD which data blocks are no longer in use.
Wear leveling is the firmware technique that distributes write operations evenly across all NAND blocks to prevent any single block from wearing out prematurely.
The Cache (computing)) article explains the general principle of caching — a smaller, faster memory tier that buffers writes.
Products like the GIGABYTE AORUS NVMe Gen4 M.2 500GB ($99.99) use an external DDR cache buffer to mitigate this, while the Acer Predator GM7000 2TB NVMe Gen4 ($264.99) uses DDR4 RAM cache to maintain
Products like the GIGABYTE AORUS NVMe Gen4 M.2 500GB ($99.99) use an external DDR cache buffer to mitigate this, while the Acer Predator GM7000 2TB NVMe Gen4 ($264.99) uses DDR4 RAM cache to maintain
Thermal throttling is a protective mechanism where the SSD controller reduces its clock speed or pauses operations when temperatures exceed safe thresholds.
Products like the SK hynix Gold S31 1TB ($97.99) are SATA-based and generate less heat, while NVMe drives like the Crucial P2 1TB ($99.99) benefit from heatsinks.
Products like the SK hynix Gold S31 1TB ($97.99) are SATA-based and generate less heat, while NVMe drives like the Crucial P2 1TB ($99.99) benefit from heatsinks.
The SSD controller must apply increasingly sophisticated Error correction code algorithms to recover data.
The BCH code and LDPC code articles explain these error correction schemes.
The BCH code and LDPC code articles explain these error correction schemes.
For example, the KINGSTON Digital SUV500MS/240G ($59.99) and Saichi mSATA SSD 256GB ($26.99) both receive periodic firmware updates that can improve long-term performance.
